title: USCIS eliminates one-year foreign residency requirement for R-1 religious workers seeking readmission
summary: USCIS issued an interim final rule amending its regulations to remove the one-year foreign residence regulatory requirement for religious workers seeking readmission to the United States. Previously, a religious worker who exhausted the five-year maximum period of stay as an R-1 religious worker was required to reside abroad and be physically present outside the United States for one year before being eligible for readmission. Under the new rule, religious workers must still depart from the United States but are no longer subject to a required period of foreign residence. USCIS stated that this change aligns with Executive Order 14205, Establishment of the White House Faith Office, and will enhance stability and significantly reduce disruptions to religious organizations providing services at churches, mosques, synagogues, and other places of worship. The interim final rule is effective January 16, 2026, and comments will be accepted until March 17, 2026.

attachments.title: USCIS Interim Final Rule - Improving Continuity for Religious Organizations and Their Employees

title: ICE authorizes forceful entries to residences based on administrative warrants
summary: ICE Acting Director Todd Lyons issued a memorandum to all ICE personnel authorizing special agents and deportation officers to execute administrative warrants to arrest noncitizens subject to final removal orders within their residences. The memorandum relies on a determination by the DHS Office of the General Counsel that nothing in the Constitution, the Immigration and Nationality Act, or immigration regulations prohibits reliance on administrative warrants for this purpose. The memo specifically authorizes ICE personnel to use force to enter a residence after knocking, announcing their presence, and providing a reasonable opportunity for the door to be opened. ICE basic training materials from 2021 state to the contrary that a warrant of removal or deportation does not alone authorize a Fourth Amendment search. The memorandum was disclosed as part of a whistleblower complaint to Congress.
